title: Brand Identity and Social Media Consistency
summary: This open workshop focused on brand identity and social media strategy for builders, designers, and contractors. Participants shared their biggest challenges around getting their brand visible online, particularly on Instagram, and Erin facilitated discussion around consistency, authenticity, and overcoming the fear of being on camera. The group also discussed structuring multiple businesses, outsourcing social media tasks, and practical tools to make content creation manageable.

teachings.explanation: Rather than claiming quality like everyone else, sharing your actual systems and processes in proposals and meetings communicates your differentiation organically. Architects, designers, and clients who witness your organized approach will spread the word even when you don't win the job.
teachings.quote: We don't get the job. Well, now the architects and designers are saying, hey, let's go work with Aaron, Aaron Riddle, because you've shared this. And then it just sort of like gets bigger and bigger.

teachings.explanation: Posting regularly on social media signals to potential clients that you are reliable and show up, which is a direct reflection of how you operate as a business. Consistency in content mirrors consistency in construction, making clients comfortable before they ever speak to you.
teachings.quote: We're just breathing consistency. So by the time they talk to us, it's already a known, it's like a given. So everybody's comfortable in the room.

teachings.explanation: Effective content goes beyond posting photos of finished work. The goal is to share a personal perspective or observation that triggers a thought or emotion in the viewer, creating resonance and connection rather than just visual display.
teachings.quote: It's like share because then people resonate. They're like, oh, I didn't even know. Like when I pick up a coffee cup, it probably should fit my hands and I have small hands. Like that's what gets triggered.

teachings.concept: The Three Stories Per Day Rule
teachings.explanation: Posting three Instagram stories per day is the foundational recipe for building visibility and staying top of mind. Stories disappear after 24 hours, which reduces pressure and keeps content fresh. Combined with one main feed post per day, this drives algorithm engagement and authentic connection.
teachings.quote: The rule is post three stories a day, no matter what, like that's how you're going to be consistent. And we post one thing a day on our Instagram.

teachings.concept: Face on Camera as Trust Builder
teachings.explanation: Showing your face in videos and stories builds personal connection and trust with potential clients far more effectively than only posting project photos. When clients can see and hear you before a first call, introductions are already made and conversations go deeper faster.
teachings.quote: When we have new business calls, it's so comfortable because they know me, but they literally know me and you skip through all the coffee talk, you know, you skip through all the BS and just get to the conversation.

teachings.explanation: Polished, edited content is less effective than raw, real, in-the-moment video. Audiences on social media, especially via stories, respond to authentic human presence over produced perfection. The thirst for human content will only grow as AI becomes more prevalent.
teachings.quote: It doesn't have to be formal, it doesn't have to be perfect. That's what I have learned. It just needs to be helpful.

teachings.explanation: When running two distinct businesses, especially when one may serve competitors of the other, keeping the brands completely separate with different names and legal entities is strategically and legally sound. It prevents brand confusion and protects each business's identity and tax structure.
teachings.quote: I have two businesses. One's the design build and I have custom closets and I have them separate and I market them separately because my custom closet business serves my competitors in the design build world.

teachings.explanation: The clients and collaborators who resonate with your authentic content are exactly the people you want to work with. Those who don't connect with your personality online are likely not the right fit anyway, making the filtering process a feature rather than a flaw of public visibility.
teachings.quote: Your people will follow you. And the people that won't, you wouldn't have wanted to work with them anyway.

actionable_advice: Post three Instagram stories every day, no matter what — they disappear in 24 hours so the pressure is low and the consistency payoff is high.
Post one main feed post per day on Instagram to maintain algorithm visibility alongside your daily stories.
Put your face on camera in your content, not just photos of your projects — people need to know who is behind the work.
Start content creation by simply filming what you find interesting on the job site or in the office and posting it without heavy editing.

If you are the bottleneck for content creation, hire a college student, a tech-savvy family member, or a virtual assistant through Upwork to handle posting — document your day and send them the clips.
Practice recording videos without the intention of posting them first to remove the pressure, then post what feels right.
Develop and share your systems and processes in proposals and client meetings so that even when you don't win the job, your professionalism spreads through the industry.
If running two businesses where one could serve competitors of the other, brand and register them as entirely separate entities with different names.
When hiring someone to manage your social media, train them on your brand voice and guidelines to ensure authenticity is maintained.
Share your Instagram handle in community channels so peers, collaborators, and potential clients can follow and engage with your content.
client_challenges: How do you differentiate your brand when everyone claims to have great quality — how do you communicate your organizational processes and systems to the market?
Impostor syndrome and uncertainty about whether personal content adds value, especially for designers wondering if showing design details like pillows is interesting enough to post.
Difficulty being consistent with social media posting — it constantly gets pushed to the back burner due to time constraints from running active projects.
Not knowing how to hand off content creation to someone else without spending just as much time briefing them as it would take to do it yourself.
Growing an Instagram following beyond people you already know — not attracting new potential clients organically.
Fear of being on camera and perfectionism causing paralysis — taking dozens of takes or abandoning content entirely before posting.
Attracting mostly peers and trade professionals on social media rather than potential end clients.
Starting two new businesses simultaneously and deciding whether to brand them together or separately.
notable_quotes: On imperfection going viral: 'I accidentally said nails. And we all know you use screws to put sheet rock in the walls, but literally within like six hours later, there were 30,000 people that were now following us.'

On overcoming bottlenecks: 'The only one getting in the way of it is you. It just — I'm the bottleneck. I know that you are. So we're going to unbottleneck this and you're going to post three stories tomorrow.'

On authenticity replacing small talk: 'When we have new business calls, it's so comfortable because they know me — you skip through all the coffee talk, you skip through all the BS and just get to the conversation.'
On the human advantage over AI: 'The AI can't pick up hands. There's going to be a thirst for the human part. So you may as well just get on it now.'
On finding your audience: 'The people that follow her are her people. And the people that won't, you wouldn't have wanted to work with them anyway.'

On practicing without pressure: 'My sister told me — just do videos like you're practicing. Like you're not going to post these. You're just practicing. And I was like, oh, okay. So I just did a bunch of practice and then I just posted them.'
